dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 4 responding for a Sick Person call at a residence in [CITY].
Hi, I'm Mike, a paramedic with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I've been throwing up and I have this sharp pain in the upper right part of my belly. It's been like that for two hours now.
Can you tell me your name?
My name is Jenny.
Where are we right now?
[CITY]... I think.
Do you know what day it is?
Yeah, it's the 13th. I don't feel good.
When I say squeeze and point, can you do that for me?
You look like you're in some pain. Can we check your airway?
Yeah... go ahead.
Your airway looks patent and you're breathing without assistance. Try not to move anything rigid on your belly unless needed.
Got the radial pulse... it is 118 and regular.
Breathe normally for me. How do you feel while breathing?
It's hard to tell with this pain, but... I don't feel like I'm gasping for air, maybe it's okay.
Respirations are 20, equal.
No abnormal sounds in your breath-bilateral and equal.
What color is your skin today?
Ugh, owing to the pain, I haven't really checked, but it's probably warm and dry.
Blood pressure is 186 over 93. That's pretty high for me, but maybe normal for someone with hypertension.
Blood glucose is at 110 mg/dL.
Earlier you mentioned vomiting. What did the emesis look like?
The earlier ones were yellow, like bile. The last one had a pink, frothy look.
What's your medical history?
I have primary hypertension, kidney stones, and a history of cholecystitis. I had a liver transplant back in 2012.
What was the first time you noticed this pain?
Around 8 PM. I was asleep and woke up with this sharp pain over my right side and started vomiting.
Have you been to the doctor recently?
Yeah, I had a meeting with my transplant doctor today.
On a scale of 0 to 10, what pain would you describe this as?
Definitely a 9.
Does the pain go anywhere at all-like the back or shoulders?
Nope, just right upper quadrant.
What does the pain feel like?
Sharp and constant.
I want to make sure you're aware of your name, location, time, and your situation. Can you tell me your name, where we are, and what day it is?
Jenny. We're in [CITY]. It's December 13th. I feel awful.
You had a GCS of 15 before we met, and you still have a 15 now.
You were doing relatively okay here. How's your skin feel like after the vomiting?
Warm and dry, just normal.
We're going to run some checks here. Can you sit and relax for a bit? We'll be placing you on the cardiac monitor.
Got the 4-lead ECG. It's showing a normal sinus rhythm so far.
IV access is secured; an 18G IV is in an arm vein.
We'll run some normal saline to help you feel better and see if it eases the nausea or vomiting. Do you mind?
Pulse has decreased to 69 and is still regular.
BP registered as 157 over 107. SpO2 is at 100% at the moment. No new drastic changes.
Right bundle branch block with a normal sinus rhythm detected in the 12-lead now.
Blood glucose now shows zero on the meter.
SpO2 now is at 99%. Everything still looks in check.
Two-provider medication cross check complete. Administering 4 mg of Zofran (Ondansetron) now IVP.
Pulse reading at 70 now, still regular.
BP is at 153 over 100. All right, that's improved from 157.
Blood glucose still is reading zero.
SpO2 remains at 99%. There was no significant drop.
Per history and symptoms, as well as the protocol for Nausea and Vomiting, we'll transport you for further evaluation.
What hospital would you prefer?
[COMPANY_NAME]. That's the one where I see my doctors.
Your stretcher is in place and we'll move you in. Use the seat belt. Relax enough to lay on the stretcher.
We've positioned you in comfort and five-point restraints are on for transport.
Hospital has been alerted en route.
Performing second set of vitals prior to transport.
Pulse is still 70 and regular.
Blood pressure is 153 over 100.
Blood glucose remains at zero.
SpO2 is at 99%.
12-lead is stable with no new findings.

We've got a 36-year-old female named Jenny coming in with a history of liver transplant and current symptoms of acute pain in the right upper quadrant with nausea and vomiting. She dry-heaved before emesis. We've initiated IV with 600mL normal saline and administered 4 mg of Zofran. Onboard vitals are stable-pulse at 70, BP at 153/100, SpO2 99%. 12-lead EKG made prior to departure.
No blood in emesis. She denies other pain or numbness. Destination chosen is [COMPANY_NAME], transport secured.
She also mentioned she had taken tylenol and some pink TUMS without improvement.
AAOx4 confirmed, with GCS of 15. Patient is alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation.
Care handed over to ER nurse now. Patient is out of our custody, formal handoff complete.
